learning_outcomes.description: Develop foundational knowledge in the area of entrepreneurship.

Understand how to develop and evaluate actionable business opportunities, including how to identify, create, evaluate and bring to market such opportunities as an effective contributor to a startup team.
Create viable and innovative Business Models to action entrepreneurial opportunities.
Critically reflect how knowledge is created in the startup process.
Recognise the inherent risks and uncertainties in the process of new venture creation.

assessments.task_description:
Task Description

Entrepreneurs rarely succeed by building products based solely on what they think customers want. Instead, they seek to understand what people genuinely value and the problems they are trying to solve in their everyday lives. This assessment develops your ability to identify opportunities that emerge from authentic customer needs. You will practice a key entrepreneurial activity: moving beyond assumptions and uncovering insights that can inform the design of products, services, and ventures that create meaningful value.

The professional practice you are developing

This assessment simulates the work of entrepreneurs, innovation managers, and product developers who must identify opportunities before designing solutions. Rather than beginning with an idea and searching for customers, you will begin with a person and seek to understand what they value, need, and are willing to change their behaviour to obtain.

What you are expected to do

Firstly, you are required to choose a problem domain in which you can ideate. While there might be some overlap when you begin to explore domains, you are required to choose one of the following four domains in the first instance.

  1. Health- This can be acute or chronic health problems, invisible disabilities, visible disabilities, mental health, etc
  2. Accessibility- This can be access to education, health services, physical access, access to financial support, access to housing
  3. Life transitions- This are major events in life that can trigger great ideas. Changing jobs, moving overseas, retiring, having a baby, back to work after having a baby, upskilling, getting your first job.
  4. Transforming places- This can be places that are not utilised or under-utilised or inaccessible to some. For example, real estate (commercial or residential), parks, stores, libraries, waterways, etc. The key question in this domain is what problem can this space solve for the communities 

Secondly, you are required to identify ten (10) individuals in your network who could potentially help you understand this problem domain in more depth. These individuals may have personal experience of this domain, work in an organisation that is situated in this domain, or an organisation that might derive future value and profit from this domain. A template will be provided to students to facilitate this process. You are required to submit this completed template at the end of Week 4 and this is worth 5% of your mark.

Thirdly, you should choose one individual from your network list and conduct one (1) customer discovery interview of at least 15 minutes and no longer than 30 minutes using Zoom or Microsoft Teams (using your UQ student account). It is vital that the recording be done in gallery mode to ensure that both the interviewer and interviewee are in frame. Here is a link that you should use to configure this correctly- recording in gallery mode. You will lose marks if you do not submit a recording where we cannot see both speakers. You will also need to upload a transcript of the interview so please ensure your Zoom settings allow this. Doing a test interview is always a good idea. This interview and transcript is worth 25% of your mark.

You should carefully consider who you interview. While it may be tempting to select a close friend, family member, or fellow student for convenience, richer entrepreneurial opportunities often emerge from engaging with individuals and organisations who face significant challenges, unmet needs, frustrations, or barriers in the problem domain. You are welcome to consult with the teaching team when selecting an interview participant to ensure that the individual is likely to generate sufficiently rich insights for entrepreneurial opportunity identification.

Interviews should be conducted in an open, empathetic, and exploratory manner with the aim of uncovering meaningful insights about the interviewee's experiences, needs, values, motivations, and challenges. Your 15-minute interview should demonstrate effective rapport building, empathy, and the ability to elicit rich customer insights. You are strongly encouraged to attend weekly workshops, where activities are designed to develop these skills and clarify course expectations for each of these dimensions.

A rubric will be made available in Blackboard and will be based on the following skills:

Build Rapport

  • Create a comfortable, trusting environment that encourages the interviewee to speak openly.                                  
  • Begin with brief conversation to establish rapport.
  • Adapt your communication style (e.g., tone, pace and formality) to suit the interviewee.
  • Demonstrate active listening through appropriate verbal and non-verbal communication.
  • Personalise the interaction while maintaining focus on the interviewee.

Demonstrate Empathy

  • Show that you understand and value the interviewee's experiences, encouraging deeper reflection and richer responses.
  • Ask predominantly open-ended questions.
  • Allow sufficient time for the interviewee to think before responding.
  • Acknowledge important emotions or experiences where appropriate.
  • Use thoughtful follow-up questions to encourage deeper reflection and elaboration.                                         

Elicit Rich and Authentic Insights

  • Gather trustworthy, meaningful insights by allowing the interviewee's experiences and perspectives to emerge without influence.
  • Encourage specific examples and experiences rather than general opinions.
  • Remain open and curious by avoiding assumptions or proposed solutions during the interview. 
  • Explore how individual experiences connect to broader social, community, or systemic contexts through neutral, non-leading follow-up questions.                  

The interview recording (in gallery mode) and transcript should be submitted via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Developing a Business Opportunity Pitch & Journey
assessments.weighting: <dd>40% 35% for pitch, 5% for Team Progress Review </dd>
assessments.due_date:
Week 12 - Week 12

Students are expected to present their pitch in their allocated workshop in Week 12
assessments.task_description:
Task Description

This assessment develops your ability to communicate and defend a business opportunity grounded in customer insight and inclusive entrepreneurship.

The professional practice you are developing

This assessment simulates the work of entrepreneurial founders who must present a business opportunity to potential investors and stakeholders. Drawing on the customer insights developed throughout the course, your team will pitch a business opportunity that addresses a meaningful customer problem and demonstrates empathetic and inclusive entrepreneurship. You will be expected to explain not only your proposed solution, but also the evidence and reasoning that led you to identify the opportunity.

What you are expected to do

You will form a team of four or five students in Week 7 and then formally self-enrol into Blackboard group. As a team, you will develop and investigate a business opportunity that creates value for a clearly identified customer group. Each team member is expected to conduct customer validation interview to contribute to this entrepreneurial process. Teams are required to submit a 'Team Progress Review' which will report on the team's meetings, interviews, roles, and emerging ideas. The Team Progress Review is worth 5 marks and is due at the end of Week 9 (Friday, 25th September at 1pm).

Your pitch should demonstrate your ability to:

  • Identify and articulate a meaningful customer problem or unmet need.
  • Explain how customer insights informed the development of the opportunity.
  • Demonstrate how the proposed solution creates value for customers.
  • Show how principles of inclusive and empathetic entrepreneurship have informed your thinking.
  • Present a coherent and viable business model.
  • Respond professionally to questions and challenges from the teaching team.

Your team will present its opportunity through:

  • A pitch presentation delivered during Week 12 workshops. The duration of the pitch will be communicated via Blackboard.
  • A completed Business Model Canvas provided as a poster or handout.
  • Participation in a question-and-answer session following the pitch.

Team roles and professional communication

Entrepreneurial ventures rely on individuals contributing in different ways. To reflect this reality, all team members are expected to contribute to the pitch process.

Teams should determine which members will deliver the pitch presentation and which members will respond to questions from investors and stakeholders. Students delivering the pitch may not participate in the Q&A session, ensuring that every team member demonstrates a professional contribution.

Assessment will consider both verbal and non-verbal communication, including the ability to communicate confidently, professionally, and persuasively to an audience.

Why this matters

The ability to communicate opportunities, justify decisions using evidence, and respond to stakeholder questions is a core capability for entrepreneurs, managers, consultants, and innovation professionals. By completing this assessment, you will practice translating customer insights into a compelling opportunity narrative and defending that opportunity under questioning—capabilities that are essential when seeking investment, organisational support, or market adoption.
assessments.submission_guidelines: Student teams will be expected to upload their presentation slides before their timetabled workshop. A link will be made available via Blackboard.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to apply for an extension.
assessments.late_submission: A penalty of 10% of the maximum possible mark will be deducted per 24 hours from time submission is due for up to 7 days. After 7 days, you will receive a mark of 0.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to apply for an extension.

assessments.assessment_title: Reflecting Upon the Theory and Practice of Entrepreneurship - Exam
assessments.task_description: Over the course of the semester, students will be expected to engage in entrepreneurial processes, to use concepts and theories that support ideation, and to develop an entrepreneurial mindset that can respond to setbacks and challenges. At the end of the semester, students will be asked to sit an examination and demonstrate their understanding of the theory of entrepreneurship as well as the practical nature of their own entrepreneurial journey in the course. More details will be provided in class.
assessments.submission_guidelines: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.deferral_or_extension: You may be able to defer this exam.
assessments.additional_info: You may be able to defer this exam.

learning_activities.topic:
What is Entrepreneurship?

What makes an entrepreneur different from a manager? And how are start-ups different from established organisations? This lecture introduces students to the foundational question: What is entrepreneurship? It explores how entrepreneurs identify and pursue opportunities under conditions of uncertainty, and how this differs from managing existing resources within stable environments.


Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: What is Entrepreneurship? What makes an entrepreneur different from a manager? And how are start-ups different from established organisations? This lecture introduces students to the foundational question: What is entrepreneurship? It explores how entrepreneurs identify and pursue opportunities under conditions of uncertainty, and how this differs from managing existing resources within stable environments.

learning_activities.topic:
Introduction to Entrepreneurship

In this workshop, students will examine how entrepreneurship is defined by experimentation, risk-taking, and resource constraints—and how this contrasts with the structures, routines, and performance metrics of more established organisations. The workshop briefly introduces key entrepreneurial traits, mindsets, and behaviours that will be revisited throughout the course.

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Introduction to Entrepreneurship In this workshop, students will examine how entrepreneurship is defined by experimentation, risk-taking, and resource constraints—and how this contrasts with the structures, routines, and performance metrics of more established organisations. The workshop briefly introduces key entrepreneurial traits, mindsets, and behaviours that will be revisited throughout the course.

learning_activities.topic:
Developing an Entrepreneurial Mindset

This lecture introduces students to the entrepreneurial mindset as a way of thinking and acting under conditions of uncertainty. It explores how entrepreneurs approach problems with creativity, resilience, and a willingness to learn through experimentation. Students will be introduced to three foundational approaches that support early-stage venture creation: effectuation, which emphasises starting with available means and adapting through co-creation; ideation, which focuses on generating and refining innovative ideas; and design thinking, which centres on empathy, prototyping, and iterative problem-solving.

Learning outcomes: L01, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Developing an Entrepreneurial Mindset This lecture introduces students to the entrepreneurial mindset as a way of thinking and acting under conditions of uncertainty. It explores how entrepreneurs approach problems with creativity, resilience, and a willingness to learn through experimentation. Students will be introduced to three foundational approaches that support early-stage venture creation: effectuation, which emphasises starting with available means and adapting through co-creation; ideation, which focuses on generating and refining innovative ideas; and design thinking, which centres on empathy, prototyping, and iterative problem-solving.

learning_activities.topic:
Developing an Entrepreneurial Mindset

In this workshop, students will apply the entrepreneurial mindset through practical activities designed to prepare them for their upcoming assessment: conducting a customer discovery interview. Using tools from ideation, effectuation, and design thinking, students will learn how to identify meaningful problems and craft thoughtful questions that uncover real customer needs.

Due to the Ekka Public Holiday, students in the Wednesday workshop are encouraged to attend an alternative workshop on Tuesday or Thursday or to review a workshop recording that will be made available especially for those students.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Developing an Entrepreneurial Mindset In this workshop, students will apply the entrepreneurial mindset through practical activities designed to prepare them for their upcoming assessment: conducting a customer discovery interview. Using tools from ideation, effectuation, and design thinking, students will learn how to identify meaningful problems and craft thoughtful questions that uncover real customer needs. Due to the Ekka Public Holiday, students in the Wednesday workshop are encouraged to attend an alternative workshop on Tuesday or Thursday or to review a workshop recording that will be made available especially for those students.

learning_activities.topic:
Customer Discovery

Understanding customers is at the heart of successful entrepreneurship. This lecture introduces the practice of customer discovery as a core method for testing assumptions and uncovering real needs. Students will learn how to move beyond idea-driven thinking to engage directly with potential users through interviews and observation.

Learning outcomes: L02, L04
learning_activities.topic_description: Customer Discovery Understanding customers is at the heart of successful entrepreneurship. This lecture introduces the practice of customer discovery as a core method for testing assumptions and uncovering real needs. Students will learn how to move beyond idea-driven thinking to engage directly with potential users through interviews and observation.

learning_activities.topic:
Customer Discovery

Rather than judging customer needs from the outside, students will learn how to uncover the often surprising, diverse, and context-specific factors that shape what people truly value. This workshop places emphasis on developing effective interview techniques, identifying early adopters, and making sense of qualitative insights. This module prepares students for their upcoming assessment by equipping them with the tools and mindset needed to conduct a meaningful customer discovery interview and apply those insights to refine their venture ideas.

Learning outcomes: L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Customer Discovery Rather than judging customer needs from the outside, students will learn how to uncover the often surprising, diverse, and context-specific factors that shape what people truly value. This workshop places emphasis on developing effective interview techniques, identifying early adopters, and making sense of qualitative insights. This module prepares students for their upcoming assessment by equipping them with the tools and mindset needed to conduct a meaningful customer discovery interview and apply those insights to refine their venture ideas.

learning_activities.topic:
Value Proposition Canvas

Great ideas fail when they don’t meet real needs. This week's lecture focusses on the Value Proposition Canvas (VPC) as a practical tool for designing offerings that align closely with customer jobs, pains, and gains. Building on earlier insights, students will critically assess the “fit” between their ideas and real customer segments.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Value Proposition Canvas Great ideas fail when they don’t meet real needs. This week's lecture focusses on the Value Proposition Canvas (VPC) as a practical tool for designing offerings that align closely with customer jobs, pains, and gains. Building on earlier insights, students will critically assess the “fit” between their ideas and real customer segments.

learning_activities.topic:
Value Proposition Canvas

In this workshop, emphasis is placed on practical application: constructing, critiquing, and iterating canvases based on actual customer discovery insights. The goal is to refine value propositions that are not only desirable, but also feasible and differentiable.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Value Proposition Canvas In this workshop, emphasis is placed on practical application: constructing, critiquing, and iterating canvases based on actual customer discovery insights. The goal is to refine value propositions that are not only desirable, but also feasible and differentiable.

learning_activities.topic:
Creating Ideas with Potential

How entrepreneurs move from personal hunches to validated concepts that others also recognise as valuable. Students will learn how to transform first-person ideas—rooted in personal experience or intuition—into third-person opportunities that resonate with real customer needs.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Creating Ideas with Potential How entrepreneurs move from personal hunches to validated concepts that others also recognise as valuable. Students will learn how to transform first-person ideas—rooted in personal experience or intuition—into third-person opportunities that resonate with real customer needs.

learning_activities.topic:
Creating Ideas with Potential

This workshop explains how entrepreneurs move from personal hunches to validated concepts that others also recognise as valuable. Students will learn how to transform first-person ideas—rooted in personal experience or intuition—into third-person opportunities that resonate with real customer needs.

Learning outcomes: L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Creating Ideas with Potential This workshop explains how entrepreneurs move from personal hunches to validated concepts that others also recognise as valuable. Students will learn how to transform first-person ideas—rooted in personal experience or intuition—into third-person opportunities that resonate with real customer needs.

learning_activities.topic:
Lean Startup Approach

Turning a validated idea into a viable venture requires more than a plan—it demands experimentation. This lecture introduces the Lean Startup approach as a disciplined method for reducing uncertainty through rapid testing and iterative development.

Learning outcomes: L02, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Lean Startup Approach Turning a validated idea into a viable venture requires more than a plan—it demands experimentation. This lecture introduces the Lean Startup approach as a disciplined method for reducing uncertainty through rapid testing and iterative development.

learning_activities.topic:
Building High Performing Entrepreneurial Teams

No scalable venture succeeds alone. This module explores how high-performing entrepreneurial teams are formed, developed, and sustained. Students will examine what makes teams effective—not just in terms of technical skills, but also cognitive diversity, shared purpose, and interpersonal trust.

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Building High Performing Entrepreneurial Teams No scalable venture succeeds alone. This module explores how high-performing entrepreneurial teams are formed, developed, and sustained. Students will examine what makes teams effective—not just in terms of technical skills, but also cognitive diversity, shared purpose, and interpersonal trust.

learning_activities.topic:
Lean Start -up

Students will learn how to develop minimum viable products (MVPs), run experiments, and make data-informed decisions. Emphasis is placed on validated learning: discovering what works (and what doesn't) before scaling.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Lean Start -up Students will learn how to develop minimum viable products (MVPs), run experiments, and make data-informed decisions. Emphasis is placed on validated learning: discovering what works (and what doesn't) before scaling.

learning_activities.topic:
Building High Performing Entrepreneurial Teams

This week students are encouraged to make a team around some shared core interests in entrepreneurship. Emphasis is placed on assembling complementary capabilities, managing roles and conflict, and how to identify gaps and cultivate the talent, networks, and culture needed for growth.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Building High Performing Entrepreneurial Teams This week students are encouraged to make a team around some shared core interests in entrepreneurship. Emphasis is placed on assembling complementary capabilities, managing roles and conflict, and how to identify gaps and cultivate the talent, networks, and culture needed for growth.

learning_activities.topic:
Organisational Support & Financing

Ideas don’t scale on passion alone. This lecture explores the critical organisational and financial resources needed to support entrepreneurial growth. Students will examine how new ventures secure external funding—from bootstrapping and angel investment to venture capital and government grants—alongside internal systems that support operational stability.

Learning outcomes: L01, L03, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Organisational Support & Financing Ideas don’t scale on passion alone. This lecture explores the critical organisational and financial resources needed to support entrepreneurial growth. Students will examine how new ventures secure external funding—from bootstrapping and angel investment to venture capital and government grants—alongside internal systems that support operational stability.

learning_activities.topic:
Organizational Support and Financing

In this workshop, emphasis is placed on aligning financing choices with business model dynamics, understanding investor expectations, and building organisational capabilities (such as governance, HR, and financial control) that enable sustainable scaling. Real-world examples and funding scenarios will help students critically assess different growth pathways.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03
learning_activities.topic_description: Organizational Support and Financing In this workshop, emphasis is placed on aligning financing choices with business model dynamics, understanding investor expectations, and building organisational capabilities (such as governance, HR, and financial control) that enable sustainable scaling. Real-world examples and funding scenarios will help students critically assess different growth pathways.

learning_activities.topic:
Business Models & Multi-sided Platforms

A strong value proposition needs a business model to match. This lecture explores how entrepreneurs design business models that create, deliver, and capture value—especially in complex, multi-sided contexts. Students will examine how platforms like Airbnb or Uber orchestrate relationships between distinct user groups, manage network effects, and monetise interactions.

Please note that there will be no corresponding workshop to this lecture due to the Labour Day public holiday in week 10. Students should make every effort to attend or review this lecture in support of Assessments 2 and 3.


Learning outcomes: L02, L03

This lecture explores how entrepreneurs design business models that create, deliver, and capture value—especially in complex, multi-sided contexts. Students will examine how platforms like Airbnb or Uber orchestrate relationships between distinct user groups, manage network effects, and monetise interactions. Please note that there will be no corresponding workshop to this lecture due to the Labour Day public holiday in week 10. Students should make every effort to attend or review this lecture in support of Assessments 2 and 3.

learning_activities.topic:
In-Semester Break

No scheduled classes this week
learning_activities.topic_description: In-Semester Break No scheduled classes this week

learning_activities.topic:
Building Legitimacy & Pitching

Ideas alone don’t persuade—legitimacy does. This lecture focuses on how entrepreneurs build credibility in the eyes of investors, customers, partners, and the public. Students will learn how to craft and deliver compelling venture pitches, but also how to strategically signal trustworthiness, competence, and alignment with audience values.Drawing on theory and practice, the lecture explores different forms of legitimacy (cognitive, moral, pragmatic) and how entrepreneurs manage impressions, narratives, and institutional expectations.

Learning outcomes: L01
learning_activities.topic_description: Building Legitimacy & Pitching Ideas alone don’t persuade—legitimacy does. This lecture focuses on how entrepreneurs build credibility in the eyes of investors, customers, partners, and the public. Students will learn how to craft and deliver compelling venture pitches, but also how to strategically signal trustworthiness, competence, and alignment with audience values.Drawing on theory and practice, the lecture explores different forms of legitimacy (cognitive, moral, pragmatic) and how entrepreneurs manage impressions, narratives, and institutional expectations.

learning_activities.topic:
Pitch Finalisation

This workshop aims to finalise their pitch presentations and select who in their team will deliver their elevation pitch according to pitching concepts. Emphasis is placed on tailoring communication to context—whether pitching for funding, forming partnerships, or building early customer trust. Students will apply these insights in a final pitch presentation, synthesising all previous activities into a clear, credible, and persuasive venture story.

Learning outcomes: L02
learning_activities.topic_description: Pitch Finalisation This workshop aims to finalise their pitch presentations and select who in their team will deliver their elevation pitch according to pitching concepts. Emphasis is placed on tailoring communication to context—whether pitching for funding, forming partnerships, or building early customer trust. Students will apply these insights in a final pitch presentation, synthesising all previous activities into a clear, credible, and persuasive venture story.

learning_activities.topic:
Entrepreneurial Purpose and Responsibility

Scaling a venture raises not only strategic questions but moral ones. This lecture explores the ethical dimensions of entrepreneurial practice—how choices around growth, funding, labour, and impact shape the kind of organisation being built. Students will critically examine their responsibilities toward customers, employees, communities, and the environment, with a particular focus on Indigenous perspectives and knowledges. Topics include ethical decision-making under uncertainty, stakeholder tensions, and the risks of extractive business models.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Entrepreneurial Purpose and Responsibility Scaling a venture raises not only strategic questions but moral ones. This lecture explores the ethical dimensions of entrepreneurial practice—how choices around growth, funding, labour, and impact shape the kind of organisation being built. Students will critically examine their responsibilities toward customers, employees, communities, and the environment, with a particular focus on Indigenous perspectives and knowledges. Topics include ethical decision-making under uncertainty, stakeholder tensions, and the risks of extractive business models.

learning_activities.topic:
Group pitches (in-class assessment)

In this workshop, student teams will present their pitch to the teaching team and fellow students. Teams should choose members of the team who will deliver the pitch and members who will answer questions posed to them.

Learning outcomes: L01, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Group pitches (in-class assessment) In this workshop, student teams will present their pitch to the teaching team and fellow students. Teams should choose members of the team who will deliver the pitch and members who will answer questions posed to them.

learning_activities.topic:
Evidence-Based Entrepreneurship

Entrepreneurship is not only a practice—it’s also a dynamic field of research. This lecture introduces students to key trends, debates, and emerging questions in entrepreneurship scholarship. From opportunity recognition and effectuation to ecosystems, Indigenous entrepreneurship, and sustainable innovation, students will explore how academic research shapes—and is shaped by—the real-world challenges entrepreneurs face.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
learning_activities.topic_description: Evidence-Based Entrepreneurship Entrepreneurship is not only a practice—it’s also a dynamic field of research. This lecture introduces students to key trends, debates, and emerging questions in entrepreneurship scholarship. From opportunity recognition and effectuation to ecosystems, Indigenous entrepreneurship, and sustainable innovation, students will explore how academic research shapes—and is shaped by—the real-world challenges entrepreneurs face.

learning_activities.topic:
Exploring your entrepreneurial purpose

This workshop aims to synthesise practical, conceptual, and ethical appraoches to entrepreneursip. The focus is on understanding how these scholarly frameworks, practical canvases, and ethical issues connect with and inform real-world practice. This workshop is critical as it will include examinable content for the final exam and help students develop skills in analytical reading and conceptual application.

Learning outcomes: L01, L04

The focus is on understanding how these scholarly frameworks, practical canvases, and ethical issues connect with and inform real-world practice. This workshop is critical as it will include examinable content for the final exam and help students develop skills in analytical reading and conceptual application.

learning_activities.topic:
From Theory to Entrepreneurial Practice- Preparation for the final exam

This lecture brings together the key concepts, tools, and experiences explored throughout the course. Students will revisit major themes—from value creation and customer discovery to ethics, financing, and legitimacy—and reflect on how these ideas were applied in workshops, team projects, and venture development. Students will be encouraged to draw on their experiential learning to demonstrate insight, critical thinking, and the capacity to apply entrepreneurial concepts in context.

Learning outcomes: L01, L02, L03, L04, L05
